briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon/evening. We will now begin today's climate briefing, April 6, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Global Climate Crisis and Urgent Call for Action
sections.content: Today, severe impacts of climate change have been reported across the globe. In Oregon, USA, a drought emergency has worsened due to a sharp decline in snowfall [11], and in Nebraska, large-scale wildfires have severely impacted ranchers [193], with extreme weather events continuing. Climate-vulnerable regions in Florida are facing new challenges [181], and research indicating that thawing permafrost emits significantly more greenhouse gases than expected adds to the severity of the situation [192]. Furthermore, the impacts of climate change are being detected across entire ecosystems, affecting the lives of dung beetles [182, 191], sea turtle shells [189], and birds at the bottom of the food chain [190]. French ski resorts are also experiencing a double hardship of climate change and funding shortages [195]. In response, experts are urging emergency measures against increasing heatwaves and climate change [196].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Accelerating Domestic Efforts for Carbon Neutrality
sections.content: The Republic of Korea is accelerating various efforts to transition to a carbon-neutral society [8]. To mark the 81st Arbor Day, tree-planting events are actively taking place across the country, including Harim [4], Gwangjin-gu [19], Daejeon Metropolitan Office of Education [20, 42, 67], KEPCO E&C [27, 31], Naju Saemaul Association [34, 54, 68], Hadong-gun [45, 91], Yongsan-gu [53, 63], Jeonju Saemaul Association [73], and Yeosu City Urban Management Corporation [75, 78], contributing to the expansion of carbon sinks. Furthermore, research teams from Korea University [12, 23] and Dong-A University [17, 25, 62] are accelerating the development of core carbon-neutral technologies, such as elucidating CO2 reduction reaction mechanisms and developing key gas separation technologies. Provinces and cities including Gyeongbuk Province [15, 29, 39, 44, 49, 51, 82], Gyeongnam Province [28, 81], Chungnam Province [32], Gumi City [33], Hadong-gun [60, 84, 86, 89], and Pohang City [90] are recruiting participants for the Carbon Neutrality Point System in the energy and automotive sectors, encouraging citizens to practice carbon neutrality. In addition, various lifestyle-oriented environmental models and educational programs are being disseminated, such as seagrass bed creation [18, 66, 92], bicycle passport initiatives [14, 41, 71], and carbon neutrality citizen education [48, 56, 57, 59, 77, 85, 87] [36, 50, 55, 70, 74, 76, 79, 80, 83].

sections.title: 🌱 Renewable Energy Transition: National Goals and Citizen Participation
sections.content: The government is fully pushing for a major energy transition centered on renewable energy [106, 110, 114, 120, 128, 153, 154, 161, 167, 168], seizing the energy crisis originating from the Middle East as an opportunity to leap forward as a renewable energy powerhouse [138, 152, 158, 172]. It has reaffirmed its commitment to expand the share of renewable energy generation to over 20% by 2030 [94, 101, 102, 104, 105, 115, 118, 135, 136, 146, 147, 176], and to achieve early the goals of supplying 100 GW of renewable energy and converting 40% of new vehicles to electric or hydrogen-powered cars [111, 115, 116, 139, 145, 149, 151, 159, 166, 175, 177]. In particular, President Lee pointed out the slow pace of energy transition and emphasized the promotion of mandatory rooftop solar power for factories and the electrification of LPG taxis [126, 156]. Various local governments, including Incheon Jung-gu [98, 113, 122, 125, 133, 137, 148, 160], Gunsan City [134, 141, 155, 165, 173], and Gwangju City [127, 169], are expanding citizen participation through support for new and renewable energy facility installation and profit-sharing schemes [123, 142, 163, 178]. Ensuring the safety of renewable energy is also emerging as a key issue for sustainability [162]. Analysis has also been presented suggesting that renewable energy acts as a buffer, mitigating international shocks caused by reliance on fossil fuels in geopolitical crisis situations [103, 179, 180].

sections.title: 🌍 Enhancing Climate Governance and International Cooperation
sections.content: News of strengthened domestic and international governance and cooperation systems for climate change response also continued. In the Republic of Korea, there is a Presidential National Climate Crisis Response Committee [1], and Yeosu City is fully committed to safety management for the successful hosting of the UNFCCC Climate Week [2, 3, 5]. The Green Forum to be hosted by Korea in 2026 is anticipated to be a key to achieving carbon neutrality [10, 65], aligning with discussions on 3rd generation NDCs (Nationally Determined Contributions for greenhouse gas reduction), which embody the meaning of global climate action [184]. Internationally, efforts to enhance citizen participation and awareness are underway, such as a climate action residency program recruiting artists in Hawaii [188] and Oregon's Whale Watch Week providing climate change information [187]. Furthermore, the fact that US nuclear power plants are exploring their roles in the new environment of climate change and AI development suggests the potential for utilizing diverse energy sources [194].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlighted global climate crisis warnings, along with active domestic and international efforts to achieve carbon neutrality and transition to renewable energy to overcome it. We must collectively build the future of climate action through continued interest and participation. This concludes today's climate briefing.
